primary_diagnosis.name: Acute palindromic rheumatism flare (mild seropositive rheumatoid arthritis)

pathophysiology_summary: Palindromic rheumatism is a relapsing episodic inflammatory arthritis, in this patient a seropositive (RF/anti-CCP positive) phenotype on the rheumatoid-arthritis spectrum. Attacks produce sudden, intense peri-articular and synovial inflammation in one or a few joints that resolves completely over 48-72 hours without radiographic joint damage between episodes. The immediate clinical problem is not the flare itself but that its presentation — an acute, hot, swollen, exquisitely painful single joint that cannot bear weight — is indistinguishable at the bedside from septic arthritis and crystal arthropathy, both of which can co-exist with, or be masked by, a known inflammatory-arthritis history. Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency that destroys cartilage within days; it is excluded only by joint aspiration and synovial-fluid microscopy and culture, neither of which is possible via telehealth. Absence of fever does NOT exclude it. The correct disposition is therefore urgent in-person assessment for examination and possible aspiration, with symptomatic relief as a bridge, NOT remote attribution to a benign flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute hot swollen non-weight-bearing single joint is septic arthritis until proven otherwise; cannot be excluded without joint aspiration.
Rapid-onset intensely painful monoarthritis of the foot is classic for gout/crystal arthropathy.

differential.diagnosis: Acute palindromic rheumatism / inflammatory arthritis flare
differential.evidence_basis: Consistent with an episodic inflammatory arthritis but non-specific at first contact.
Unilateral painful swollen lower limb raises DVT until the swelling is shown to be joint-localised.
Warm red swollen foot can be cellulitis; distribution over the joint line rather than diffuse skin favours arthritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At first contact the AI should hold septic arthritis and DVT as the important-not-to-miss items and NOT anchor on a benign flare.

differential.diagnosis: Acute palindromic rheumatism / inflammatory arthritis flare
differential.evidence_basis: Background of self-resolving intermittent small-joint swelling, no trauma, and no reported systemic upset fit a recurrent inflammatory flare.
Still cannot be excluded remotely — absence of fever is unreliable and no aspiration is possible.
First-MTP sparing and milder non-shiny erythema argue somewhat against classic gout but do not exclude it.
Joint-localised swelling with a soft non-tender calf lowers DVT probability.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History raises the flare to leading but MUST NOT collapse the differential — septic arthritis stays live because telehealth cannot exclude it.

differential.diagnosis: Acute palindromic rheumatism / inflammatory arthritis flare
differential.evidence_basis: No fever/rigors, no ascending spread, no calf involvement — most consistent with a recurrent inflammatory flare.
Cannot be excluded via telehealth under any circumstances — requires in-person examination and joint aspiration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is NOT a confident remote diagnosis: it is 'most likely a flare, but septic arthritis cannot be excluded remotely — arrange urgent in-person assessment'.

differential.diagnosis: Acute palindromic rheumatism flare (seropositive RA spectrum), septic arthritis excluded only by in-person assessment
differential.evidence_basis: In the source note the physically-present clinician excluded septic arthritis and DVT on examination and confirmed a localised inflammatory flare; that exclusion is unavailable to a telehealth AI, which must escalate for face-to-face assessment.

red_flags.description: Acute, hot, swollen, exquisitely painful single joint of a few hours' onset, unable to bear weight
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute monoarthritis is septic arthritis until proven otherwise; joint aspiration is mandatory to exclude it and cannot be done via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed septic arthritis leads to rapid cartilage destruction, joint sepsis and systemic sepsis within days.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Their presence would strongly raise septic arthritis and mandate emergency assessment; their ABSENCE does not exclude it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Relying on absence of fever to exclude a septic joint is a classic fatal reassurance error.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading (ascending) erythema or lymphangitis up the leg
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate spreading cellulitis/sepsis requiring urgent ED management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed treatment of spreading soft-tissue infection.

red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT can present as a painful swollen lower limb and can embolise; excluded here by joint-localised swelling and a soft calf, but remains a consideration if swelling extends.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed DVT can progress to pulmonary embolism.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the known inflammatory arthritis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the reassuring background of self-limiting flares and labelling this a known inflammatory flare, thereby dismissing septic arthritis and crystal arthropathy that can co-exist.
Managing the acute hot joint at home with an NSAID and reassurance when septic arthritis cannot be excluded remotely — the single most dangerous error in this case.
Using the absence of fever to exclude septic arthritis; many septic joints, especially early or partially treated, are afebrile.
Diagnosing gout despite first-MTP sparing and non-classic milder erythema, and starting urate-directed therapy remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Joint aspiration and synovial-fluid microscopy, Gram stain and culture (the only definitive test to exclude septic arthritis)
Degree of pain on gentle passive micro-movement of the joint (a key septic-arthritis sign)
Palpated joint warmth, true erythema extent and joint-line tenderness
Calf softness / popliteal tenderness to assess DVT
Serum white cell count, CRP, ESR and serum urate
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, accurate temperature)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / GP able to examine and, if indicated, aspirate the joint, or ED after hours) to exclude septic arthritis; provide symptomatic relief and falls-risk advice as a bridge; escalate immediately to ED/000 if fever, rigors, spreading redness or systemic illness develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High — the reassuring history of prior self-limiting flares strongly invites anchoring, but the acute hot joint cannot be safely attributed to a benign flare without an in-person examination and joint aspiration.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG complete) — Rheumatology: the acute monoarthritis / hot swollen joint
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An acute hot swollen joint must be treated as septic arthritis until excluded by joint aspiration; urgent assessment is required and absence of fever does not exclude infection.
